stem:
The following text is from a short article on urban gardening.

Community gardens in dense city neighborhoods do more than produce vegetables. Researchers studying a network of gardens in Philadelphia argue that these plots serve as informal gathering places where neighbors who might otherwise never speak form lasting friendships.

Which quotation from a resident would most directly support the researchers' claim?

choices.text: "I grow tomatoes here because they taste better than the ones at the supermarket."
"I met three of my closest friends pulling weeds next to me on Saturday mornings."
"The soil in our plots needed a lot of work before anything would grow."
"The city charges us a small annual fee to keep the water running."

explanation: The claim is that gardens help neighbors form friendships; the quotation about meeting close friends while gardening is the only option that directly evidences that social outcome.

stem:
The following text is from a review of a children's novel.

In her debut novel, Ana Reyes tells the story of a shy girl who discovers courage through a summer spent on her grandmother's farm. The reviewer argues that the book's strength lies in how gradually the main character's confidence grows over the course of the story.

Which detail from the novel would most directly support the reviewer's claim?

choices.text: The grandmother's farmhouse is described as weathered but welcoming.
The grandmother tells stories about her own childhood on the farm.
The book includes detailed descriptions of local birds and plants.
In the first chapter the girl refuses to enter the barn; by the final chapter she leads a lost calf inside it without hesitation.

explanation: The claim concerns gradual growth in confidence; the contrast between early fear of the barn and later fearless leadership directly shows that change over time.

stem:
The following text is adapted from a science magazine.

Biologist Renata Costa studies how octopuses solve problems in captivity. She has proposed that octopuses can remember which human caretakers have treated them well and which have not, and that they behave differently toward each.

Which finding, if true, would most directly support Costa's proposal?

choices.text: Octopuses have more neurons in their arms than in their central brain.
In a controlled study, octopuses consistently squirted water at a caretaker who had previously annoyed them while ignoring a caretaker who had fed them, even when both wore identical uniforms.
Captive octopuses sometimes escape their tanks at night when no one is watching.
Octopuses in the wild change color to match their surroundings in under a second.

explanation: Choice C shows octopuses distinguishing between specific individuals based on prior treatment, which is exactly what Costa's proposal requires.

stem:
The following text is from a local news story.

A town council member has argued that the new bike lane on Willow Avenue has made the street safer for everyone, not just cyclists. She points to data collected in the six months since the lane was installed.

Which piece of data would most directly support her argument?

choices.text: The number of bicycles sold in local stores increased during the six-month period.
Total reported traffic collisions on Willow Avenue fell by 42 percent, including a drop in collisions involving only cars.
Several business owners reported that the bike lane looked attractive to visitors.
Construction of the bike lane was completed ahead of schedule and under budget.

explanation: The claim is that the lane made the street safer for everyone; a drop in all collisions, including car-only ones, directly supports that broader safety benefit.

stem:
The following text is from an article about exercise habits.

Fitness researcher Daniel Park argues that walking in a group is more likely to become a lasting habit than walking alone. He has studied hundreds of adults over the course of a year.

Which finding would most directly support Park's argument?

choices.text: Walking is one of the most commonly recommended forms of exercise for adults over 50.
Adults who walked with a regular group were still walking weekly after one year at more than twice the rate of those who walked alone.
Group walkers often choose longer routes than solo walkers.
Most participants in the study owned comfortable walking shoes.

explanation: Park's claim is specifically about lasting habit; the higher one-year retention rate for group walkers is the only option that measures sustained behavior.

stem:
The following text is from a literary essay.

Scholars of Gabriel García Márquez have long debated which element most defines his fiction. Critic Luisa Ibarra contends that what truly unites his work is not the fantastical imagery, as is often claimed, but an insistence that ordinary domestic life deserves the same mythic weight as grand historical events.

Which observation about García Márquez's novels would most directly support Ibarra's claim?

choices.text: His characters frequently speak with animals and witness miraculous occurrences.
His novels have been translated into more than forty languages and sold millions of copies worldwide.
He was born in a small town in Colombia and drew inspiration from his grandparents' stories.
He often devotes page-long passages to the careful preparation of a family meal, framing the scene in the same elevated prose he reserves for wars and revolutions.

explanation: Ibarra's specific claim is that García Márquez elevates domestic life to mythic status; the example of meal preparation written in the same style as wars is a direct match.

stem:
The following text is adapted from an economics article.

Economist Priya Varma has argued that raising the minimum wage in a particular mid-sized city did not reduce overall employment, contrary to predictions made before the increase. She bases this conclusion on data gathered two years after the wage went up.

Which finding from the data would most directly support Varma's conclusion?

choices.text: Workers in the city who kept their jobs reported higher take-home pay after the increase.
Consumer prices in the city rose slightly compared with the previous year.
Some small business owners publicly complained about the new wage law.
The total number of employed people in the city was statistically unchanged two years later, with similar numbers in nearby cities that did not raise their wages.

explanation: Varma's claim is specifically about overall employment; stable total employment after the increase, matched against comparable cities, directly addresses that outcome.

stem:
The following text is from a review of a contemporary play.

In her play *The Quiet Room*, playwright Juno Okafor depicts a family whose members have stopped speaking to one another over a years-old dispute. A reviewer claims that Okafor uses staging, rather than dialogue, to convey the depth of the family's estrangement.

Which detail from a production of the play would most directly support the reviewer's claim?

choices.text: Each family member sits in a separate pool of light on opposite sides of the stage, never crossing into another's space, even during scenes in which they share a meal.
The play opens with a long monologue in which the father lists every grievance he holds against his children.
A running time of two and a half hours with no intermission.
The set includes family photographs hung on the back wall of the living room.

explanation: The reviewer emphasizes staging over dialogue; separated lighting pools that prevent physical interaction is a purely staging-based choice that communicates estrangement without words.

stem:
The following text is from an environmental science report.

Ecologist Samuel Greene has proposed that reintroducing beavers to a degraded stream in the Pacific Northwest would, within a decade, increase the number of fish species present in that stream. His proposal has been controversial among local fisheries managers.

Which finding, if true, would most directly support Greene's proposal?

choices.text: Beavers build dams that can cause flooding of nearby farmland.
Fisheries managers in a neighboring region have expressed skepticism about beaver reintroduction.
A similar stream where beavers were reintroduced eight years ago now contains six fish species that were absent before the reintroduction.
Beaver populations in North America have recovered significantly since the 1970s.

explanation: The claim is about beavers causing an increase in fish species within a decade; a documented case of a comparable stream gaining six species within eight years after reintroduction is the direct evidence.

stem:
The following text is from an article on memory and aging.

Neuroscientist Hae-Won Lim argues that older adults are not worse than younger adults at remembering information, but rather better at filtering out details that are unlikely to be useful later. Some colleagues have disputed this interpretation.

Which finding would most directly support Lim's argument?

choices.text: Older adults are more likely than younger adults to report feeling confident in their memory.
Brain imaging studies show some structural differences in the hippocampus between younger and older adults.
Adults of all ages perform worse on memory tests when they have not slept well the night before.
Older adults in one study remembered fewer random words from a list than younger adults, but when the list was described as medically important, their recall matched that of younger participants.

explanation: Lim's specific claim is about selective filtering, not general decline; older adults matching younger ones when the information is labeled useful supports that their recall depends on relevance, not raw ability.

stem:
The following text is adapted from a historical essay.

Historian Malia Kahananui argues that eighteenth-century Hawaiian navigation relied on a body of knowledge as precise and systematic as contemporary European celestial navigation, despite being transmitted orally rather than in written charts. Some earlier historians had dismissed Polynesian wayfinding as largely intuitive.

Which finding would most directly support Kahananui's argument?

choices.text: Modern Hawaiian voyagers, trained only through oral chant and memorized star patterns from surviving practitioners, have successfully navigated between Hawaii and Tahiti without instruments.
European navigators of the same era occasionally consulted Pacific Islanders for help with local winds.
Hawaiian oral tradition includes many stories about voyaging ancestors.
Eighteenth-century European ships were larger than the canoes used by Hawaiian voyagers.

explanation: Kahananui's claim is that the oral knowledge was precise and systematic enough to function like instrument navigation; the successful modern voyages based purely on that oral knowledge directly demonstrate that reliability.

stem:
The following text is from an article on urban planning.

Planner Theo Saldanha claims that the key to reducing traffic congestion in a large city is not building more roads but making public transit more frequent during off-peak hours. He has based his argument on case studies from several cities.

Which observation from one of Saldanha's case studies would most directly support his claim?

choices.text: A city that widened three highways saw average rush-hour commute times return to their pre-expansion levels within four years.
A city that doubled the frequency of midday and evening bus service recorded a 19 percent drop in peak-hour car trips within two years, without adding any new road capacity.
Residents in one case-study city reported that they preferred riding trains to taking buses.
Most cities in the study had some form of public transit before any changes were made.

explanation: Saldanha's claim pairs off-peak transit improvement with congestion reduction; the specific case of doubled off-peak service cutting peak-hour car trips without road expansion is a direct match.

stem:
The following text is from a review of a memoir.

In his memoir *Returning Home*, Raúl Mendoza writes about leaving engineering to become a potter in the village where he grew up. A critic argues that Mendoza's real subject is not the craft of pottery at all, but the way physical labor can restore a sense of identity lost to years of abstract office work.

Which passage from the memoir would most directly support the critic's argument?

choices.text: A description of the specific type of clay found near Mendoza's village and the minerals that give it its distinctive color.
A chapter recounting the history of pottery in Mendoza's region going back several centuries.
A scene in which Mendoza argues with a buyer about the price of a set of bowls.

A paragraph in which Mendoza writes, "It was only when my hands began to ache from the wheel that I finally recognized myself again; I had been a stranger to my own body for twenty years behind a desk."

explanation: The critic's claim links physical labor to regained identity; Mendoza's own statement that manual work let him recognize himself after years of office work directly evidences exactly that connection.

stem:
The following text is from a psychology research summary.

Psychologist Ingrid Bauer has proposed that people are more willing to cooperate with strangers when they believe they will see those same strangers again in the future, even if no reward is offered for cooperation. Critics have argued that without a material reward, repeated contact alone should not change behavior.

Which finding from an experiment would most directly support Bauer's proposal?

choices.text: Participants reported that they generally liked the other people in the experiment.
Participants who were paid a higher show-up fee played more rounds of the game overall.
Experimenters noticed that participants often chatted with one another before the game began.

Participants told they would play a single anonymous round of a sharing game with a stranger gave away an average of 18 percent of their tokens, while participants told they would play ten more rounds with the same stranger gave away an average of 46 percent in the first round, even though no rewards were offered.

explanation: Bauer's claim ties expected future contact to higher cooperation absent rewards; the large jump in first-round giving when participants expected future rounds, with no rewards involved, matches each part of that claim.

stem:
The following text is adapted from an article about agriculture.

Agronomist Chidinma Obi argues that a traditional crop-rotation method used for generations in parts of West Africa produces comparable yields to modern chemical-fertilizer approaches while better preserving soil over the long term. Some agricultural companies have questioned whether the traditional method can keep up with modern demands.

Which finding would most directly support Obi's argument?

choices.text: Farmers using the traditional method report that it is part of their cultural heritage.
Chemical fertilizers have been widely adopted in some neighboring countries.
The traditional method requires farmers to plant a wider variety of crops than the fertilizer approach does.
Over a fifteen-year study, fields farmed with the traditional rotation matched fertilizer-treated fields in yield per hectare while retaining 38 percent more topsoil.

explanation: Obi's claim has two specific parts: comparable yield and better soil preservation; the fifteen-year study matching yields while retaining more topsoil addresses both directly.

stem:
The following text is adapted from a literary study.

Literary scholar Henrietta Vogel contends that Emily Dickinson's dashes are not, as earlier editors assumed, a casual feature that can be replaced with conventional punctuation without loss. Vogel argues specifically that the dashes function as rhythmic markers that force a reader's voice to hesitate in ways that reshape the meaning of a line.

Which finding would most directly support Vogel's specific claim?

choices.text: Dickinson's handwritten manuscripts show that she used dashes of varying lengths, and early editors sometimes replaced them with commas or periods.

In a controlled reading study, participants who read Dickinson poems in their original dashed form paused at the dashes and reported different interpretations of several lines than participants who read the same poems with dashes replaced by commas.
Dickinson wrote nearly 1,800 poems, most of which were not published during her lifetime.
Several twentieth-century poets have cited Dickinson's punctuation as an influence on their own work.

explanation: Vogel specifies both a rhythmic pause and a resulting change in meaning; only the reading study directly shows both the hesitation at the dashes and the shift in interpretation when they are replaced.

stem:
The following text is from an archaeology article.

Archaeologist Bjorn Haldorsen argues that a cluster of 9,000-year-old stone structures in northern Europe was not, as commonly assumed, used primarily for storing food, but instead served as a site for seasonal community gatherings at which groups from distant regions met to exchange goods and information. Skeptics note that many similar sites elsewhere were used for storage.

Which finding would most directly support Haldorsen's specific argument?

choices.text: Excavations at the site have uncovered stone tools and animal bones consistent with prehistoric human occupation.

Objects recovered from within the structures include beads, flint, and shell ornaments whose raw materials originate from four different regions up to 600 kilometers away, with almost no residue of stored grain or preserved meat.
Radiocarbon dating confirms that the structures are approximately 9,000 years old.
The structures are larger than most other storage sites known from the same period.

explanation: Haldorsen's argument is twofold: not used for storage and used instead for long-distance exchange; materials from four distant regions combined with an absence of food residue directly evidences both parts.

stem:
The following text is adapted from an article in a music journal.

Musicologist Femi Adetola has proposed that the polyrhythmic patterns characteristic of West African drumming traditions influenced early jazz not, as some have argued, through a general cultural inheritance, but through specific, traceable rhythmic motifs that appeared first in recordings by a small number of New Orleans drummers in the 1910s and then spread rapidly through the genre.

Which finding would most directly support Adetola's specific proposal?

choices.text: Musicological analysis of 1910s recordings from a handful of named New Orleans drummers identifies three distinct rhythmic motifs matching documented West African patterns, and shows those same motifs appearing in recordings by dozens of other jazz drummers within the next decade.
Early jazz musicians in New Orleans performed in ensembles that often included drums, horns, and piano.
Survey responses from modern jazz musicians indicate that many feel a general cultural connection to African rhythms.
Several biographies of early jazz musicians mention ancestors who had come from West Africa.

explanation: Adetola's proposal requires traceable motifs, a small starting group, and rapid spread; the analysis identifies specific matching motifs in a small set of drummers and then documents their spread, satisfying each part.

stem:
The following text is from a public health paper.

Epidemiologist Marta Lindqvist has argued that a sustained decline in childhood asthma rates in one Scandinavian city over a twenty-year period cannot be explained by reductions in outdoor air pollution alone, as some officials have claimed. She contends that improvements in indoor housing conditions, particularly the reduction of dampness and mold, were the larger factor.

Which finding would most directly support Lindqvist's specific contention?

choices.text: Average outdoor particulate levels in the city fell by roughly 20 percent over the twenty-year period.

When the city's asthma decline is compared with that of three neighboring cities, the neighboring cities showed similar reductions in outdoor pollution but no comparable decline in asthma; all three, however, also saw far smaller reductions in home dampness and mold than the study city.
Public health officials in the city ran several campaigns encouraging families to keep children away from smoky areas.
Asthma is known to be affected by both outdoor and indoor air quality.

explanation: Lindqvist's specific claim is that outdoor pollution alone cannot explain the decline and that indoor dampness matters more; comparison cities with similar pollution drops but no asthma decline and less indoor improvement isolates indoor conditions as the distinguishing factor.

stem:
The following text is from a film studies essay.

Film scholar Isabela Rocha argues that the director Chantal Akerman's decision to use extremely long, static takes in her 1975 film *Jeanne Dielman* was not merely a stylistic preference but a deliberate ethical choice: the takes force the viewer to experience the duration of domestic labor in real time, rather than seeing it compressed as is typical in film. Other critics have maintained that the long takes simply reflect the avant-garde sensibilities of the period.

Which piece of evidence would most directly support Rocha's specific argument?

choices.text: Akerman's early films were screened at festivals associated with 1970s avant-garde cinema.
The film was shot in a small Brussels apartment over the course of several weeks.

A 1976 interview in which Akerman states, "I wanted the audience to sit through the peeling of every potato because I wanted them to feel, not just see, how much of a woman's day is taken up by tasks no one watches; cutting any of it would have been a lie."
Several other directors in the 1970s experimented with unusually long takes in their own films.

explanation: Rocha's claim is that Akerman's long takes were an ethical choice meant to make viewers experience domestic labor in real time; Akerman's own statement that any cutting would be a lie directly voices that ethical motive.
